text: Epictetus uses the metaphor of an actor to explain what is ours. The role — hero or beggar, long or short — is assigned by the author of the play. Our business is to perform whatever role we are given with full commitment and skill. The actor who refuses a minor role on the grounds that he deserves a grander one has confused the performance with the performer. What is ours is the quality of the performance; what is not ours is the script. This metaphor extends to social roles: the Stoic does not resent being born poor, ill, or obscure, because excellence of character is available in every role equally.
question: In Epictetus's actor metaphor, what specifically belongs to 'what is ours'?

options.text: Choosing which role to play in the performance
The quality and commitment of the performance itself
Determining the length and content of the play
Selecting the audience that will witness the performance
